title: Write Inequalities
contentObjective: I can write inequalities to represent real-world situations.
languageObjective: I can explain my inequality using the words inequality, greater than, less than, and at least / at most.

turnAndTalk.question: A witness says the suspect is 'at least 18 years old,' written as a ≥ 18. Why does the detective use an inequality here instead of an equation like a = 18?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The age could be 18 or any number greater, so it is not a single value.

stems.en: An inequality fits better than an equation because ___.
stems.es: Una desigualdad encaja mejor que una ecuación porque ___.

stems.en: 'At least 18' means the age can be ___.
stems.es: 'Al menos 18' significa que la edad puede ser ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students explain that 'at least 18' allows many values (18 and up), so an inequality with ≥ captures it while an equation would force one value.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would the meaning change if the clue used a > 18 instead of a ≥ 18? Justify whether an 18-year-old still fits.
extendStems: The line under the symbol means ___.

turnAndTalk.question: How do the phrases 'at least' and 'at most' change which inequality symbol you choose compared to 'more than' and 'fewer than'?
turnAndTalk.kernel: 'At least' and 'at most' include the number; 'more/fewer than' do not.

stems.en: 'At least' uses ___ because it ___ the number, but 'more than' uses ___.
stems.es: 'Al menos' usa ___ porque ___ el número, pero 'más que' usa ___.

stems.en: My inequality is ___ because the phrase says ___.
stems.es: Mi desigualdad es ___ porque la frase dice ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students match 'at least' to ≥ and 'at most' to ≤ (include the value), and 'more than'/'fewer than' to > and < (exclude the value).
turnAndTalk.extend: Compare 'no more than 8 exits' with 'fewer than 8 exits.' Could 8 exits be allowed in each case? Justify.
extendStems: 8 exits is allowed with ___ but not with ___ because ___.
The difference is whether the number is ___.

turnAndTalk.question: Where do you see inequalities in real life, such as age limits or speed limits?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Many real rules describe a range of allowed values, not one value.

stems.en: I see an inequality when ___, and I would write ___.
stems.es: Veo una desigualdad cuando ___, y escribiría ___.

stems.en: This is an inequality, not an equation, because ___.
stems.es: Esto es una desigualdad, no una ecuación, porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students give a real example (ride height, speed limit, ticket age) and write an inequality with the correct symbol for a range of values.
turnAndTalk.extend: Critique: 'A speed limit of 25 mph means you must drive exactly 25.' Write the correct inequality and explain the real rule.
extendStems: This is wrong because a speed limit means ___.

vocabulary.definition: A math sentence that compares two sides with <, >, ≤, or ≥.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una oración matemática que compara dos lados con <, >, ≤ o ≥.
vocabulary.visual: x > 5 means x can be 6, 7, 8, ... — any number greater than 5
vocabulary.cloze: A math sentence comparing values with <, >, ≤, or ≥ is an ___.

examples.why: It compares values with a greater-than sign.
That is an equation, not an inequality.

vocabulary.definition: The > sign, showing one number is bigger.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El signo >, que muestra que un número es mayor.
vocabulary.cloze: The symbol > that means one value is larger than another means ___.

vocabulary.definition: The < sign, showing one number is smaller.
vocabulary.definitionEs: El signo <, que muestra que un número es menor.
vocabulary.cloze: The symbol < that means one value is smaller than another means ___.

vocabulary.visual: 'At least 10 players' → p ≥ 10 (could be 10, 11, 12, ...); 'At most 5 tries' → t ≤ 5 (could be 5, 4, 3, ...)
vocabulary.cloze: 'At least' means greater than or equal to, and 'at most' means ___ than or equal to.

vocabulary.definition: A letter that stands for an unknown number.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una letra que representa un número desconocido.
vocabulary.visual: In x > 5, the letter x stands for any number greater than 5.
vocabulary.cloze: A letter that stands for an unknown number is a ___.

vocabulary.definition: A phrase meaning less than or equal to (≤).
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una frase que significa menor o igual que (≤).
vocabulary.cloze: The phrase that means less than or equal to is "no more than," written with the ___ symbol.

launch.narrative: Detective Navarro is narrowing down suspects. A witness says the suspect is at least 18 years old. The detective writes the inequality a ≥ 18, where a is the suspect's age. How does this help eliminate suspects?
launch.contextImage: Detective whiteboard with suspect profiles and age constraints circled
noticePrompts: What does 'at least 18' mean about the suspect's age?
How is an inequality different from an equation?
wonderPrompts: What if the witness said the suspect was less than 30 years old?
Could you combine two inequalities to narrow it down even more?

explore.instructions: Match each phrase to the correct inequality symbol. Drag each phrase card into the matching symbol category.

categories.label: ≥ (greater than or equal to)
items: No fewer than 15 pieces of evidence

hints: Key words: 'more than' and 'over' mean >. 'Fewer than' and 'under' mean <.
'At least' and 'no fewer than' mean ≥. 'At most' and 'no more than' mean ≤.
explore.label: Sort each phrase into the inequality symbol it represents.

discourse.prompt: How do the words 'at least' and 'at most' change the inequality symbol compared to 'more than' and 'fewer than'?
discourse.sentenceFrame: 'At least' uses ≥ instead of > because it means the value ___ be equal to the number, while 'more than' means it must be ___.

optional.stem: Which inequality means 'a number x is at least 10'?
Which inequality means 'you must be more than 5 feet tall to ride'?
A bus holds at most 40 people. Which inequality shows the number p of people allowed?
Which inequality means 'a number n is less than 8'?

approaching.stem: Which inequality represents: 'The temperature is less than 40 degrees'?
approaching.explanation: 'Less than' means <, so the inequality is t < 40.

approaching.stem: Which inequality represents: 'You must be at least 48 inches tall to ride'?
approaching.explanation: 'At least' means greater than or equal to, so h ≥ 48.

approaching.stem: Which inequality represents: 'A store can hold no more than 75 people'?
approaching.explanation: 'No more than' means less than or equal to, so p ≤ 75.

approaching.stem: Which inequality represents: 'The speed limit is more than 25 mph'?
approaching.explanation: 'More than' means strictly greater than, so s > 25.

approaching.instructions: Place markers on the number line for each value, then decide: is 5 > 3 true or false? Is 2 ≥ 7 true or false?
hints: On a number line, greater numbers are to the right. 5 is to the right of 3, so 5 > 3 is true.
2 is to the LEFT of 7, so 2 ≥ 7 is false.

onLevel.label: Match each situation to its inequality

pairs.situation: A suspect weighs more than 150 pounds.
The detective can spend at most $200 on equipment.
The crime happened fewer than 3 hours ago.
The vault requires at least 5 digit codes.

rows.situation: A package weighs less than 50 pounds.
You need at least $25 to buy the game.
No more than 30 students can join the club.

onLevel.label: Identify the key phrase, symbol, and write the inequality for each situation.

onLevel.instructions: Sort each inequality into whether x = 10 is a solution.
hints: Substitute 10 for x and check if the statement is true.
Remember: ≥ means 10 counts (10 ≥ 10 is true), but > means 10 does not count (10 > 10 is false).
onLevel.label: Test x = 10 in each inequality. Sort by whether it works.

onLevel.stem: A parking garage charges if your stay is more than 2 hours. Which inequality represents times t that are charged?
onLevel.explanation: 'More than 2 hours' means strictly greater than: t > 2. Exactly 2 hours is not charged.

extending.title: Find the Inequality Error

workedExample.label: Situation
workedExample.work: No more than 25 students can join the field trip

extending.correctWork: 'No more than' means less than or equal to (≤), not greater than. The correct inequality is s ≤ 25.
hints: 'No more than 25' means the number cannot exceed 25. Is that greater than or less than or equal to?
'No more than' = at most = ≤. The correct inequality is s ≤ 25.

extending.prompt: Write two different real-world situations: one that uses > and one that uses ≥. Explain why the situations need different symbols even though both mean 'bigger.'
extending.sentenceFrame: Situation 1 (>): ___. This uses > because ___. Situation 2 (≥): ___. This uses ≥ because ___. The difference is that ≥ includes ___ while > does not.

rows.situation: A detective has more than 12 cases.
rows.why: 'More than' means many possible values → inequality

rows.situation: A detective has at most 12 cases.
rows.why: 'At most' means 12 or fewer → inequality with ≤

extending.label: For each situation, decide if it needs an equation or inequality and explain why.

connect.scenario: A detective knows the getaway car was traveling at most 65 miles per hour on the highway. The inequality s ≤ 65 represents the car's speed s.
connect.promptQuestion: What speeds are possible for the getaway car? How did the phrase 'at most' help you write the inequality?
connect.prompt: The car's speed could be ___ or ___ because 'at most 65' means the speed is ___ 65.

exitTicket.stem: Which inequality represents: 'A number n is at least 14'?
exitTicket.explanation: 'At least 14' means 14 or greater, so n ≥ 14.
